title: Exponential distribution MLE
text: demonstrate R package algebraic.mle, consider relatively simple case random sample ..d. exponentially distributed random variables. First, load R package algebraic.mle, along R packages need, :

title: Exponential distribution MLE
text: define parameters ..d. random sample : generate random sample \(X_i \sim \operatorname{EXP}(\lambda=1)\) \(=1,\ldots,n\) : observed sample size \(n=1000\). show observations sample (data frame) : show histogram sample overlaid plot exponential function’s pdf, :
code: n <- 1000 rate <- 1 x <- tibble(x=rexp(n,rate)) print(head(x,n=4)) #> # A tibble: 4 × 1 #>       x #>   <dbl> #> 1 0.408 #> 2 1.19  #> 3 0.251 #> 4 0.641 ggplot(x, aes(x=x)) + geom_histogram(aes(y=..density..),alpha=.2) +     xlim(0,6) +      geom_function(fun=dexp)

title: Exponential distribution MLE
text: like estimate \(\lambda=1\), can using maximum likelihood estimation implemented algebraic.mle package: can show point estimator : can show Fisher information variance-covariance matrices : (rate.hat vector, vcov output variance-covariance matrix. may consider outputs \(1 \times 1\) matrices.) can show confidence interval :
code: rate.hat <- mle_exp(x$x) summary(rate.hat) #> Maximum likelihood estimator, of type mle , #> is normally distributed with mean #> [1] 0.9762777 #> and variance-covariance #>              [,1] #> [1,] 0.0009531182 #> --- #> The asymptotic mean squared error 0.0009531182  #> The asymptotic 95% confidence interval is #>       2.5 %   97.5 % #> 1 0.9254968 1.027059 #> The log-likelihood is -1024.008  #> The AIC is 2050.016 point(rate.hat) #> [1] 0.9762777 fisher_info(rate.hat) #>          [,1] #> [1,] 1049.188 vcov(rate.hat) #>              [,1] #> [1,] 0.0009531182 confint(rate.hat) #>       2.5 %   97.5 % #> 1 0.9254968 1.027059

title: Exponential distribution MLE
text: general, estimate sampling distribution, generate \(B=10000\) samples (size \(1000\)) corresponding estimators, \(\hat\theta^{(1)},\ldots,\hat\theta^{(B)}\). Normally, \(B\) samples, , gather \(B\) samples one sample (used weighted MLE), contain (Fisher) information \(\theta\). However, nice property MLEs , asymptotically, converge normal distribution mean given true parameter, case \(\lambda\), variance-covariance given inverse Fisher information matrix \(\), case \((\lambda) = n/\lambda^2\), .e., \(\hat\lambda \sim N(\lambda,^{-1}(\lambda))\). observe empirical sampling distribution \(\hat\theta\) overlaid theoretical asymptotic distribution :  know \(\lambda\), may estimate sample, thus may approximate sampling distribution \(\hat\lambda\) \(N(\hat\lambda,^{-1}(\hat\lambda))\). Since giving one sample, provide \(B\) estimates \(\lambda\). However, can sample approximation asymptotic distribution \(\hat\lambda\) : visually compare two MLE samples, data0 data1, :  Due sampling error, see approximation, estimate asymptotic sampling distribution \(N(\hat\lambda,\hat\lambda/n^{1/2})\), shifted left sample true distribution, appear quite similar otherwise.
code: B <- 1000 data0 <- numeric(length=B) for (i in 1:B) {     x <- stats::rexp(n,rate)     data0[i] <- point(mle_exp(x)) } ggplot(tibble(rate.hat=data0), aes(x=rate.hat)) +     geom_histogram(aes(y=..density..),alpha=.3,bins=50) +     geom_function(fun=function(x) { dnorm(x,mean=rate,sd=rate/sqrt(n)) }) data1 <- sampler(rate.hat)(B) data <- data.frame(values=c(data0,data1), group=c(rep("empirical",B),rep("approximation",B)))  ggplot(data,aes(x=values,fill=group)) +     geom_histogram(aes(y=..density..),position="identity", alpha=0.2, bins=50)

title: Exponential distribution MLE
text: interesting property MLE \(\hat\lambda\) MLE \(f(\lambda)\) given \(f(\hat\lambda)\). method rmap applied object x (x,"mle") == TRUE function f compatible point(x) (optionally simulation sample size) computes MLE f(x).

previous_headings: Maximum likelihood estimation > Invariance property of the MLE
title: Exponential distribution MLE
text: know MLE \(\hat\lambda \sim N(\lambda,\lambda^2/n)\). seek transformation \(g(\hat\lambda)\) expectation \(2 \lambda\), .e., \(g(\lambda) = 2\lambda\): compute MLE \(2 \hat\lambda\) (using simulation size \(n=1000\)) : Note simulation size estimating \(f(\lambda)\); regardless large large \(n\) , MLE \(f(\hat \lambda)\) sampling error , best, function sampling error \(\hat\lambda\). , \(\operatorname{var}(f(\hat\lambda)) = 4 \lambda^2/n\). Letting \(\lambda=1\), see , asymptotically, \(g(\hat\lambda) \sim N(2,4/n)\). expected mean \(2\) variance \(4/n = 0.004\), pretty close obtained:
code: f <- function(lambda) 2*lambda f.hat <- rmap(rate.hat,f,n=1000) point(f.hat) #>          [,1] #> [1,] 1.952555 vcov(f.hat) #>             [,1] #> [1,] 0.003828861

what: Weighted MLE: a weighted sum of maximum likelihood estimators
title: Exponential distribution MLE
text: Since variance-covariance MLE inversely proportional Fisher information MLE defined respect , can combine multiple MLEs \(\theta\), may defined respect different kind sample, arrive MLE incorporates Fisher information samples. Consider \(k\) mutually independent MLE estimators parameter \(\theta\), \(\hat\theta_1,\ldots,\hat\theta_k\), \(\hat\theta_j \sim N(\theta,I_j^{-1}(\theta))\). , maximum likelihood estimator \(\theta\) incorporates data \(\hat\theta_1,\ldots,\hat\theta_k\) given inverse-variance weighted mean, \[     \hat\theta = \left(\sum I_j(\theta)\right)^{-1} \left(\sum I_j(\theta) \theta_j\right). \]

previous_headings: Weighted MLE: a weighted sum of maximum likelihood estimators
title: Exponential distribution MLE
text: evaluate performance weighted MLE, generate sample \(N=1000\) observations \(\operatorname{EXP}(\lambda=1)\) compute MLE observed sample, denoted \(\theta\). divide observed sample \(r=5\) sub-samples, size \(N/r=100\), compute MLE sub-sampled, denoted \(\theta^{(1)},\ldots,\theta^{(r)}\). Finally, weighted combination MLEs form weighted MLE, denoted \(\theta_w\): show results following table: see \(\hat\theta\) \(\hat\theta_w\) model approximately sampling distribution \(\theta\).
code: N <- 1000 r <- 5 samp <- rexp(N) samp.sub <- matrix(samp,nrow=r) mles.sub <- list(length=r) for (i in 1:r)     mles.sub[[i]] <- mle_exp(samp.sub[i,])  mle.wt <- mle_weighted(mles.sub)  mle <- mle_exp(samp) df <- data.frame(     c(point(mle.wt),point(mle)),     c(vcov(mle.wt),vcov(mle)))  colnames(df) <- c("point estimate","variance") rownames(df) <- c("weighted MLE", "MLE") print(df) #>              point estimate     variance #> weighted MLE      0.9608463 0.0009283218 #> MLE               0.9661501 0.0009334461

title:
algebraic.mle: A package for algebraically operating on and generating
maximum likelihood estimators from existing maximum likelihood estimators. — algebraic.mle
text: follows, briefly define API (generic functions, mostly) default implementations objects inherit mle. vcov(mle) returns variance-covariance matrix model parameter estimates. point(mle) returns point maximizes likelihood model. confint(mle) returns confidence intervals true parameter value estimated mle object. sampler(mle) maps function may used sample sampling distribution mle object. mse(mle) computes asymptotic mean squared error mle object. mle_weighted(mles) computes weighted maximum likelihood estimate independent mle objects list argument mles. fisher_info(mle) returns Fisher information matrix model's parameters. rmap(mle,g) provides numerical_mle maximum likelihood estimator g(mle). (Note numerical_mle inherits mle.) mle_linear_transform(,mle) provides closed-form solution linear Finally, since normal distributions closed linear transformations, letting p--q matrix x q dimensional mle object, prod(,x) p dimensional multivariate normal mean prod(,x) variance-covariance prod(tr(),vcov(x),. Furthermore, invariance property MLE, prod(,x) MLE prod(,true(x)) true(x) (usually unknown) parameter estimated x. define set generic functions multiplying mle objects (non-random) matrices vectors permit sort operations, asymptotic distributions operators applied mle objects exactly known asymptotic distribution mle objects exactly known.
